questions.question: Explain the difference between static and non-static data members in C++.
keyPoints: Static data members are shared among all objects of a class
Non-static data members have separate copies for each object
Static members exist as single copy for entire class
Non-static members are allocated for each object created

questions.question: Write the syntax for declaring and defining a static data member in C++.
keyPoints: Declaration inside class: static int count;
Definition outside class: int MyClass::count = 0;
Must be defined exactly once
Can be accessed using ClassName::member

questions.question: Why cannot static member functions access 'this' pointer? Explain.
keyPoints: Static member functions belong to class, not to any object
'this' pointer points to the current object
Since static functions don't operate on objects, no 'this' exists
They can only access static members directly

questions.question: Write a C++ program to count the number of objects created for a class using static data member.
keyPoints: Define static int objectCount in class
Increment in constructor
Decrement in destructor
Display count using class name or object

questions.question: Explain the various characteristics of static member functions in C++.
keyPoints: Belong to class, not to any object
Can be called without object using ClassName::function()
Cannot access 'this' pointer
Cannot be virtual
Can access only static data members directly

questions.question: What is a static local variable? Explain its characteristics.
keyPoints: Variable declared inside a function with static keyword
Retains value between function calls
Initialized only once (first function call)
Has static storage duration - exists for entire program lifetime
Allocated in data segment, not stack

questions.question: Mention the restrictions imposed on static member functions in C++.
keyPoints: Cannot be virtual
Cannot be const or volatile
Cannot use 'this' pointer
Cannot access non-static members directly
Cannot have same signature as non-static function

questions.question: Explain the concept of static class members in C++. Discuss the differences between static data members and static member functions with suitable examples.
keyPoints: Static data members: shared, single copy for class, declared inside class, defined outside
Static member functions: belong to class, callable without object, no 'this' pointer
Differences: static data holds values, static functions perform operations
Example showing object counter with static data
Example showing static function to access static data
Access: ClassName::member or object.member

questions.question: Write a complete C++ program that demonstrates the use of static data members to maintain a count of how many objects of a class have been created. Include constructor, destructor, and a static member function to display the count.
keyPoints: Class definition with static int count
Constructor increments count
Destructor decrements count
Static member function to display count
Main creates and destroys objects
Shows count changing

questions.question: Explain where static members are stored in memory. What are the advantages and disadvantages of using static members in C++?
keyPoints: Stored in data segment (global memory)
Not in object memory or stack
Advantages: share data, memory efficient, can track class-wide info
Disadvantages: global state can cause coupling, thread safety issues, harder to test
Use cases: counters, configuration, singleton pattern

questions.question: Discuss the rules for initialization of static data members in C++. Also explain what happens if static members are not initialized and mention about static const members.
keyPoints: Must be defined exactly once outside class
If not defined: linker error (undefined reference)
C++11 allows static const integral/enum initialization inside class
Static const requires definition for non-integral types
Initialization syntax: type ClassName::member = value;
In-class initialization only for certain types in C++11+
